name: HMS P 38 (P 38)
events: The history of HMS P 38 as compiled on this page is extracted from P 38's patrol reports and logbook (only December 1941 is available) and was created in October 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in May 2026.

16 Oct 1941 Around 0815 A/16, P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with P 612 / Murat Reis (Lt.Cdr. W.S. Hall, RN). They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)
17 Oct 1941 Around 1635 A/17, P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow.
On arrival at Holy Loch she was taken over from the shipyard. Acceptance trials had been successfully completed.
She was now to commence a work-up period. (2)

7 Nov 1941 At 2000 A/7, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was to patrol in the North Atlantic to the west-south-west of Ireland for an anti-Uboat patrol. She was to patrol in area enclosed by 50°00'N / 51°00'N and 15°00'W / 17°00'W.
During passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) until position 342° - Bishop Rock - 7 miles where they parted company at 2050 A/9.
En-route to her patrol area she was to proceed, dived by day, through position 50°00'N, 15°00'W.
[As no log is available for this period and as the patrol report does not give daily positions, no map can be displayed.] (3)

14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1316 A/14, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); To avoid shipping movements proceed forthwith submerged by day to patrol within five miles of position 50°50'N, 16°48'W. You will be ordered to leave your patrol after dark/16. (4)

15 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 2030 A/15, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); A) Leave patrol after dark/16 proceeding through position 50°50'N, 16°48'W diving by day further routed as follows; I) 180° - Bishops Rock - 8 miles. II) 180° - Lizard Head - 16 miles. III) 50°05'N, 02°00'W IV) 50°27'N, 00°24'W to make rendezvous with the escort in position 50°31'N, 00°41'W at 0800 A/21. B) Bombing restrictions had been arranged as follows; I) 20 Miles either side of a line joining 50°50'N, 16°48'W and 180° - Bishop Rock - 8 miles between 16°48'N, 11°00' from 1800 A/16 to 2000 A/18 and between 11°00'W and 06°30'W from 0001 A/18 to 1900 A/20. II) In area 51, east of 06°30'W from 0001 A/19 to 1900 A/20. III) In area 53, from 0001 A/20 to 1900 A/20. IV) in area 41, from 0001 A/20 to 1100 A/21. C) Bishop Rock light will be shown for 10 minutes every half hour from 0500 A/19 to daylight. (4)
15 Nov 1941 At 0230 A/15, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) arrived in position 50°50'N, 16°48'W and commenced to patrol within 5 miles from this position. (3)
16 Nov 1941 At 1940 A/16, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) surfaced and proceeded eastwards in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.5's signal timed 2030 A/15. (3)

17 Nov 1941 At 0015 A/17, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN), lost her officer of the watch overboard. T/S.Lt. M.H. Christopher, DSM, RNVR was washed overboard in very bad weather and could not be found.
At 0140 A/17, the search was abandoned and HMS P 38 continued eastwards.

At 2330 A/17, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2202 A/17 was received which contained the following orders; Proceed forthwith submerged by day through the following positions; A) 48°00'N, 10°30'W. B) 47°30'N, 09°00'W. C) 49°00'N, 06°00'W. D) Thence to Portsmouth as ordered by the Capt. S.5.
In a signal timed 2214 A/17, from the F.O.S. HMS P 38 was informed of the following; Aircraft reconnaissance shows Prinz Eugen dock almost certainly flooded. (3)

20 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0901 A/20 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); I) From position 49°00'N, 06°00'W proceed through position 50°00'N, 02°00'W and 50°27'N, 00°24'W to make rendezvous with the escort in position 50°31'N, 00°41'W at 0800 A/22. II) Submerged bombing restrictions will be in force in area 53 from 2100 A/20 to 1100 A/22 and area 41 from 0600 A/21 to 1100 A/22. (3)
22 Nov 1941 At 1200 A/22, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) arrived in the ordered rendezvous position but the escort was not in sight.
At 1415 A/22, course was altered along the 20 fathom sounding line.
At 1552 A/22, the trawler escort [identity not known] was sighted.
At 1825 A/22, HMS P 38 anchored in St. Helens Roads. ended her 1st war patrol at Portsmouth. (3)
23 Nov 1941 Around 0915 A/23, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) ended her first war patrol at Gosport (Portsmouth). (3)
4 Dec 1941 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) conducted diving trials in the Solent. (5)

7 Dec 1941 At 1345 A/7, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for her 2nd war patrol. She was to patrol to the south of Ireland on an anti-Uboat patrol. She was to patrol in an area bounded by the parallels of 49°00'N / 50°00'N and the meridians of 13°00'W / 15°00'W.

She was routes as follows; By Q.Z.S. 284 and Q.Z.S. 293 [swept mine zones] to position 50°31'N, 02°01'W and thence through the following positions; A) 50°19'N, 02°11'W. B) 180° - Start Point - 20 miles. C) 180° - Lizard Head - 16 miles. D) 49°30'N, 13°00'W.
HMS P 38 was swept out until 1645 A/7 by HMS Hector Franz [unable to find any info on a vessel with this name].
For the daily positions of HMS P 38 during this patrol see the map below.
11 Dec 1941 At 0200 A/11, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area. (3)

14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1050 A/14, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Leave patrol after dark 15th through position 49°30'N, 13°00'W diving by day routed through positions; A) 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles. B) 180° - Lizard Head - 25 miles. C) 180° - Start Point - 25 miles to make rendezvous with the escort in the vicinity of Start Point at 0930 A/19, keeping west of position 'C' until 0600 A/19. (6)
15 Dec 1941 At 1915 A/15, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) surfaced and proceeded eastwards in accordance with the Capt. S.5's orders contained in his signal timed 1050 A/14. (3)

17 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2050 A/17, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Proceed forthwith to Plymouth to refuel and complete with provisions as quickly as possible. Make rendezvous at the end of Q.Z.S. 278 [swept channel] not earlier than 0900 A/18, keeping 10 miles off Lizard Head. (6)

18 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0001 A/18, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) sent the following signal; Capt. S.5 signal timed 2050 A/17. My position is 49°40'N, 06°56'W. course 085°. E.T.A. outer end Q.Z.S. 278 1100 A/18.

In a signal timed 0245 A/18, the Capt. S.5 sent the following to HMS P 38; My 2050 A/17. Complete your passage on the surface at your discretion and make rendezvous with trawler HMS Cornelian (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S. Gorrell, RNR) at 1000 A/18 at the end of Q.Z.S. 278.
At 1045 A/18, HMS P 38 made rendezvous with the escort.
At 1300 A/18, HMS P 38 ended her 2nd war patrol at Plymouth. (3)

20 Dec 1941 At 1640 A/20, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Cherbourg, France in case the German capital ships at Brest might make a run up the Channel towards Germany.
P 38 was to proceed from the end of Q.Z.S. 278 [swept channel] through position 180° - Start Point - 15 miles to patrol in the vicinity of position 49°53'N, 01°47'W.
She was escorted out by HMS Ellesmere (T/Lt. A.C. Munro, RNVR). (3)
20 Dec 1941 For the daily position of HMS P 38 during her 3rd war patrol see the map below.

21 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1000 A/21, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS H 32 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Leave patrol after dark tonight routed as follows; A) H 32 on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through for Falmouth. Keep east of 04°15'W, until daylight. B) P 38 through position 50°00'N, 01°45'W to rendezvous in position 50°31'N, 00°41'W at 0845 A/22 for Portsmouth. C) Bombing restrictions has been arranged.

In a follow up signal timed 1749 A/21, the Capt. S.5 signalled the following to HMS P 38; 1) HMS Kingston Chrysoberyl (T/Skr. L. Ford, RNR) and HMS Kingston Topaz (Skr. W.M. Smith, RNR) will be at rendezvous at 0845 A/22. 2) Sanctuary in force from 0745 A/22 to 1200 A/22. 3) Keep south of 50°25'N, until 0730 A/22.

In a follow up signal timed 1751 A/21, the Capt. S.5 signalled the following to HMS H 32; 1) HMS Loch Park (T/Skr. R.H. Maulkinson, RNR) will be at the rendezvous at 1200 A/22. 2) Convoy PW 84 is expected at Falmouth at 1100 A/22. No other movements affect you. (6)
22 Dec 1941 Around 1240 A/22, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Gosport (Portsmouth).
She was escorted in by HMS Kingston Chrysoberyl (T/Skr. L. Ford, RNR) and HMS Kingston Topaz (Skr. W.M. Smith, RNR) which had joined around 0850 A/22. (3)
23 Dec 1941 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) is docked in AFD 2 at Gosport (Portsmouth). (5)
26 Dec 1941 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) is undocked and performed noise trails in the Solent. (5)
5 Jan 1942 At 1430 A/5, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN), departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar. She was escorted out by HMS Kingston Jacinth (Skr. A.G. Day, RNR) until 1730 A/5.

HMS P 38 was routed as follows; Q.Z.S. 111 and Q.Z.S. 169 (swept channels), 50°12'N, 01°18'W, 49°40'N. 05°00'W, 49°20'N, 11°00'W, 46°00'N, 13°00'W, Thence on the surface, due in position 43°00'N, 13°00'W at 1700 A/10 (In a signal timed 1651 A/7 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) this position and time were changed to 43°00'N, 11°30'W) at 0800 A/10. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.).
HMS P 38 was to proceed to Malta where she was to join the 10th Submarine Flotilla based there. (7)
5 Jan 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 38 during the passage from Portsmouth to Gibraltar see the map below.

7 Jan 1942 At 0300 A/7, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) received the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 0016 A/7 which contained the following orders Proceed submerged by day through position 48°30'N, 07°20'E W to patrol in position 44°41'N, 07°52'W (north of Cape Ortegal). Bombing restriction are arranged in areas 52 and 54 both west of 06°30'W forthwith until further notice.
At 2130 A/7, the signal timed 1651 A/7 from the F.O.S. was received in which the above orders were cancelled. (8)

9 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1138 A/9, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Onward route through the following positions; Position J = 38°00'N, 11°00'W. Position K = 36°40'N, 09°10'W. Position L = 36°30'N, 07°00'W. Make rendezvous with the escort in Position M = 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles at 0830 A/14. If unable to comply within two hours report when past position J. Dive by day until south of 42°00'N. Bombing restrictions have been arranged. (8)

12 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1506 A/12, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic informed HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) of the following; HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, DSO, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN) is expected to arrive at Gibraltar 0830 A/14 from the north-westward. Speed 21.5 knots. (8)

13 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 2128 A/13, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, DSO, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) of the following; There may be strong concentration of U-Boats in the western approaches to the Strait of Gibraltar. (8)
14 Jan 1942 At 1725 A/14, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Portsmouth.
She was escorted in by HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, RN) which had joined around 1400 A/14. (7)
19 Jan 1942 Around 0900 A/19, HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
It is possible that on departure exercises were carried out.
[As no log and / or passage report are available for this period no map can be displayed. Also some details regarding this passage may be missing.] (9)
29 Jan 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. (9)

8 Feb 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet in area 'G', 36°05'N, 11°20'E - 000° - 35 - 60. En-route through this area she was to proceed through position 36°05'N, 12°35'E. (10)

9 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1217 B/9, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN) and HMS Fortune (Lt.Cdr. R.D.H.S. Pankhurst, RN) proceeding high speed through positions 36°41'N, 11°10'E and 36°23'N, 12°28'E to position 36°12'N 13°32'E passing the second position about 0700 B/11 thence by the south-east swept channel. P 38 is to keep south of 36°15'N and west of 12°20'E until 1200 B/11. (11)

14 Feb 1942 It must have been HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) that torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Ariosto (4115 GRT, built 1902) off the coast of Tunisia. 138 Allied POW's out of 294 aboard are lost as well as 26 Italians out of 116 (crew and passengers).
Ariosto was in convoy together with the German transport Atlas (2297 GRT, built 1927). They were escorted by the Italian destroyer Premuda and the Italian torpedo boat Polluce.

At 1600 B/13, the Italian transport Anna Maria (1205 GRT, built 1912) had sailed from Tripoli unescorted. Atlas sailed from Tripoli at 1630 B/13. The torpedo boat Polluce sailed from Tripoli and escorted her taking the lead to scout ahead. Ariosto and Premuda departed at 1740 B/13.

At 0306 B/14, flares had been dropped and Ariosto was attacked by a bomber. This was a Swordfish piloted by Sub. Lt. Helliwell (830 Squadron), but Premuda had covered the target with a smoke screen and opened fire with its anti-aircraft guns. The aircraft was unable to locate the target and did not drop its torpedo.

At 0835 B/14, the Anna Maria joined the Atlas / Polluce section. At 1040 B/14, they were joined in turn by Ariosto and Premuda. It is not clear when Anna Maria left them but she is no longer mentioned in the reports by the three other ships. Her destination was Palermo.
At 1917 B/14, the two sections separated again with Ariosto and Premuda taking the lead.

At 2215 B/14, Ariosto was struck by a torpedo but did not sink immediately. The Atlas section which had not heard the explosion rejoined. Atlas proceeded alone while Premuda and Polluce looked for survivors.
Ariosto was carrying 116 crew members and military passengers (90 survived), and 294 PoW's (162 survived). There were thus 158 victims in all (132 PoW's).
At 1042 B/15, Polluce caught up with Atlas, and they arrived at Palermo at 0847 B/16.
At 1431 B/15, Premuda received the order from Supermarina to proceed to Trapani, and she arrived there at 1340 B/15.

16 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1810 B/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Sokol proceed through positions 33°20'N, 12°55'E and 33°20'N, 13°45'E to position 'W', 32°41° 14°32'E. P 38 proceed through positions 35°00'N, 12°25'E and 33°38'N, 13°45'E to position 'V' 32°48'N, 14°34'E. It is vitally important to intercept the expected convoy.
In a signal 1151 B/16, the Capt. S.10 had already ordered HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) had been ordered to patrol in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E).
HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) were ordered to leave Malta to patrol in positions 'Y', (32°48'N, 14°11'E) and 'Z', (32°46'N, 14°22'E) respectively.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (11) 17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (11) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1125 B/20 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Leave patrol P.M./20 to arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) at 0715 B/22. Proceed through positions 35°00'N, 14°10'E and 35°30'N, 14°12'E. (11) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow. The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B). Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy. To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys. At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona). At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron. At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay. At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23. At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22. At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24. At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy. Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23. Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23. The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24. The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (11) 21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (11) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (11) 23 Feb 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) is ordered to return to Malta. (10) 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (11) 25 Feb 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) failed to arrive at Malta as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10. (10) 26 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1937 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following order to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Report your position. Return to the Allied Warships section
The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (11)

17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (11)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1125 B/20 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Leave patrol P.M./20 to arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) at 0715 B/22. Proceed through positions 35°00'N, 14°10'E and 35°30'N, 14°12'E. (11)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow.

The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).
At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron.
At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay.

At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23.

At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22.
At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24.

At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy.

Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23.

Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24.
The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (11)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (11)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (11)
23 Feb 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) is ordered to return to Malta. (10)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (11)
25 Feb 1942 HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) failed to arrive at Malta as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10. (10)
26 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1937 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following order to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN); Report your position. (11)
